did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid AI model combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) for feature extraction and Random Forest (RF) for classification to perform skin lesion classification (melanoma, benign nevi, seborrheic keratosis) on the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a hybrid CNN-RF model for skin lesion classification. It details specific methods (data preprocessing, model architecture, hyperparameter tuning), reports quantitative results (accuracy 89.5%, AUC 0.92) on the ISIC dataset, and includes a conclusion based on these new findings. Although published in a conference proceedings (AUTOCOM), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Deep Learning Meets Traditional AI: A Hybrid CNN-RF Model for Accurate and Explainable Skin Lesion Classification
journal: 2025 International Conference on Automation and Computation (AUTOCOM)
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid AI model combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) for feature extraction and Random Forest (RF) for classification to address skin lesion classification tasks, specifically distinguishing melanoma, benign nevi, and seborrheic keratosis. The model was evaluated on the ISIC dataset using dermoscopic images, with preprocessing steps including resizing, normalization, and data augmentation to handle class imbalance. The hybrid approach outperformed standalone CNN and RF models, achieving an accuracy of 89.5%, precision of 88.9%, recall of 89.2%, and an F1 score of 89.0%. Additionally, the model demonstrated strong discriminative power with an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.92 and improved interpretability through Grad-CAM visualizations and RF feature importance analysis. The results indicate that the hybrid CNN-RF model provides a robust and accurate solution for automated skin lesion classification with better generalization than individual models.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three classes in the ISIC dataset used for classification; identified as the most aggressive and deadliest form of skin cancer.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion categories (melanoma, benign nevi, seborrheic keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesions using a hybrid CNN-RF model on the ISIC dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma, benign nevi, seborrheic keratosis) with reported accuracy of 89.5%, precision of 88.9%, recall of 89.2%, and F1 score of 89.0%.

datasets_used.name: ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collaboration) dataset
datasets_used.description: A public benchmark dataset consisting of dermoscopic images of multiple types of skin lesions. The study utilized this dataset for evaluating the proposed hybrid CNN-RF model.

use.training: Used for training the hybrid CNN-RF model, including preprocessing steps like resizing, normalization, and data augmentation to handle class imbalance.
use.validation: Used for validation to monitor learning behavior and ensure generalization, with stable training and validation accuracy curves reported.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of model performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, AUC) and confusion matrix analysis.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the model's discriminative power and compare performance against standalone models.
To analyze classification performance by counting true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives for each class.
To tune hyperparameters of the Random Forest classifier (e.g., number of estimators, maximum tree depth) to optimize performance and reduce overfitting.
To handle class imbalance and improve model generalization during the preprocessing phase.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the hybrid CNN-RF model and standalone models were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, AUC) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collabo.notes: The hybrid CNN-RF model outperformed standalone CNN and RF models. The model showed stable learning with decreasing training and validation loss and growing training and validation accuracy curves, indicating good generalization and lack of strong overfitting. Qualitative analysis using Grad-CAM visualizations and RF feature importance confirmed interpretability. Confusion matrix analysis showed decreased misclassification rates with evenly distributed performance on all lesion classes (melanoma, benign nevi, seborrheic keratosis).

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning with a pre-trained ResNet-50 model for feature extraction; fine-tuned on the ISIC dataset.
architecture_used.input_size: Resized to a uniform scale compatible with the CNN model (specific dimensions not reported).

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Deep features extracted by removing the last fully connected layer of the CNN.
architecture_details.rf_hyperparameters: Optimized via grid search for number of estimators and maximum tree depth.

Data augmentation to handle class imbalance and improve generalization

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during preprocessing to handle class imbalance and improve model generalization. Specific geometric or color transform parameters (e.g., rotation angles, flip probabilities) were not reported in the text.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid CNN-RF model outperforms standalone CNN and RF models in skin lesion classification, achieving an accuracy of 89.5%, precision of 88.9%, recall of 89.2%, and an F1 score of 89.0%.
The hybrid model demonstrates strong discriminative power with an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.92, surpassing standalone models.
The integration of CNN-based feature extraction with Random Forest classification enhances generalization and reduces overfitting compared to individual models.
The model exhibits stable learning with decreasing training and validation loss and growing accuracy curves, indicating robustness and lack of strong overfitting.
The hybrid approach improves interpretability through Grad-CAM visualizations for lesion region localization and RF feature importance analysis for clinical decision support.
The study acknowledges limitations of standalone CNNs, including high computational cost, susceptibility to overfitting on small/imbalanced datasets, and lack of interpretability.
The authors note that effective generalization of deep models typically requires large-scale labeled datasets, which are often limited and imbalanced in dermatology.
The hybrid model is presented as a promising solution for automated skin lesion classification with potential applications in tele-dermatology, mobile health, and decision support systems.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC dataset for evaluation, lacking external validation on independent cohorts or diverse skin tones, which limits generalizability to real-world clinical populations.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of performance differences between the hybrid model and standalone baselines.

Specific details regarding the data split (training/validation/testing proportions) and exact class distribution counts are not reported, raising potential concerns about data leakage or selection bias.
Data augmentation techniques were applied to handle class imbalance, but specific parameters (e.g., rotation angles, flip probabilities) are not disclosed, limiting reproducibility.
The paper acknowledges that annotated skin lesion data is inherently limited and imbalanced, which is a known source of bias in dermatological AI models.
The model was evaluated only on three specific lesion types (melanoma, benign nevi, seborrheic keratosis), limiting its clinical relevance for broader dermatological differential diagnosis.

As a conference paper, the depth of ablation studies and hyperparameter sensitivity analysis is limited compared to journal publications, potentially obscuring the robustness of the reported 89.5% accuracy.
